title: Moving to the Netherlands With Kids
metaTitle: Moving to the Netherlands With Kids: Schools, Childcare & Family Planning
subtitle: What expat families often consider when relocating with children.
description: A practical overview of moving to the Netherlands with children, including housing, schools, childcare, registration, healthcare, and everyday family life.

heroCta.supportingText: Relocating with children often means extra planning for schools, childcare, housing, registration, and healthcare. Use the family checklist and 90-day planner to organize what applies before and after arrival.

quickAnswers.value: Families relocating to the Netherlands with children, whether all move together or in stages.

sections.heading: Overview
body: Moving to the Netherlands with kids usually adds planning in four areas: documents, housing, school or childcare, and family administration after arrival. Many steps are still similar to solo or couple relocation, but family moves often require more coordination and earlier planning.
This page gives a practical overview of what expat families often need to think through before the move, during arrival, and in the first months after settling in the Netherlands.

links.label: Moving to the Netherlands with family
Moving to the Netherlands with a partner
Documents needed to move to the Netherlands

sections.heading: Relocating to the Netherlands as a family
body: Family moves often involve the same core admin steps as individual moves, but for multiple people. Housing suitability matters more because address registration, school access, commuting, and neighborhood fit are all more important. Some families move at the same time; others move in phases. Family documentation may be more complex if children need birth certificates, school records, vaccination records, or translated documents. School and childcare planning often need to start before arrival.
bullets: Housing that works for the full family

sections.heading: Documents families often prepare
body: Families often prepare a broader document pack than solo movers. Depending on your route, the school type, and municipality requirements, you may need identity documents, civil records, education records, and healthcare-related documents.
bullets: Passports or IDs for all family members
Marriage certificate or partnership proof if relevant
Proof of address or housing confirmation
Permit or sponsor documents where applicable
Apostilles or certified translations if required

callout.title: Document needs vary
callout.text: Document needs can vary by relocation route, school type, and municipality. Prepare originals and digital copies early.

links.label: Documents needed to move to the Netherlands
Moving to the Netherlands with family

sections.heading: School considerations for expat families
body: School planning is often one of the biggest family relocation decisions. The right choice depends on child age, language, location, budget, and how long you expect to stay in the Netherlands.

ctaBlock.title: Plan schools, housing, and admin together
ctaBlock.supportingText: Use the Family Moving Checklist to organize school research, housing, documents, and early arrival setup in one place.

services.name: International School of Amsterdam
services.description: International school option commonly considered by expat families in the Amsterdam region.

services.name: The British School of Amsterdam
services.description: British curriculum school often considered by relocating international families in Amsterdam.

sections.heading: Understanding the Dutch school system
body: Children in the Netherlands usually start primary school around age 4. Families often choose between Dutch public education, international schools, or sometimes bilingual or alternative options depending on the city and the child's background. Dutch public schools are usually the lower-cost route. International schools can be significantly more expensive. Placement depends on location and availability. Language adaptation matters.

sections.heading: Childcare and daycare
body: Families with younger children often need childcare planning before or soon after arrival. In the Netherlands, daycare and after-school care can be essential for working parents, but waiting lists and cost can both matter. Full-time childcare can be expensive before subsidies. Costs vary by location, provider, and days used.

services.name: Partou
services.description: Dutch childcare provider with daycare and after-school care in multiple locations.
services.indicativeCost: Childcare pricing varies by location, hours, and subsidies.

services.name: Kinderopvang.nl
services.description: Childcare directory useful when researching daycare and after-school options in the Netherlands.

sections.heading: Housing considerations for families
body: Family housing often requires more planning because location, size, registration suitability, school proximity, and transport options all matter at the same time. Temporary housing can help while researching schools and neighborhoods, but confirm whether registration is possible if you need it for early admin.
bullets: Address registration for all family members

links.label: Moving to the Netherlands with family

services.name: HousingAnywhere
services.description: Temporary housing platform often used by internationals before securing longer-term family accommodation.

sections.heading: Arrival administration with children
body: Once a family has a usable address in the Netherlands, many practical arrival steps begin to look similar to those for individuals — but they now need to be completed for the household. Some family admin can happen quickly after arrival; other parts, such as school placement or childcare, may take longer depending on city and availability.
bullets: Municipality registration for all family members
Childcare or after-school planning if relevant

ctaBlock.title: Organize the first months after arrival
ctaBlock.supportingText: Use the 90-day planner to map registration, health insurance, school steps, and family routines after the move.

links.label: Register your address in the Netherlands

services.indicativeCost: Fees vary by transfer and usage

services.name: Simyo

sections.heading: Healthcare for children
body: Families often need to understand how healthcare works for children soon after arrival. In many cases, children are linked to a parent's health insurance arrangement, but practical setup still includes finding a local doctor and understanding how referrals and routine care work.
bullets: Keeping school / childcare health documents ready

services.name: Independer
services.description: Compare Dutch health insurance policies once family registration and timing are clearer.

sections.heading: Child benefits and family allowances
body: Some families may qualify for family-related benefits or allowances once they are living in the Netherlands and meet the relevant conditions. These can depend on residency, income, child age, childcare usage, and your broader household situation. Do not assume entitlement — confirm current conditions with official sources.

callout.title: Planning guidance only
callout.text: Eligibility depends on your situation. Confirm current conditions and requirements with the SVB or other official sources.

sections.heading: Settling into family life
body: After the first weeks, families often shift from arrival admin into routines that make daily life easier and more predictable.

sections.heading: Practical family planning checklist
body: Family moves are easier when documents, housing, and school planning are tracked together rather than separately.
bullets: Confirm the relocation route for the full household
Gather identity and civil documents for all family members
Research neighborhoods with school and transport fit
Decide whether temporary housing is needed first
Plan registration for all family members
Understand health insurance and doctor setup
Map your first 30–90 days as a family

faq.q: Can children register with the municipality in the Netherlands?
faq.a: Children are usually registered together with their parents when the household registers an address. The whole family can typically register at the same address; confirm the exact process with your municipality.

faq.q: Do children need their own BSN in the Netherlands?
faq.a: Yes. Children usually receive a BSN after being registered in the municipal records. Each family member gets their own BSN, which is often needed for school, healthcare, and benefits.

faq.q: Can children attend Dutch public schools if they do not speak Dutch?
faq.a: It depends on the school and municipality. Many Dutch schools have language support or newcomer programmes; others may have different requirements. International and bilingual options exist in various cities. Check with schools in your area for their approach.

faq.q: Are international schools expensive in the Netherlands?
faq.a: International schools can be significantly more expensive than Dutch public education. Tuition varies by school, grade, and location. Budget accordingly and compare options early.

faq.q: How early should we arrange childcare?
faq.a: Waiting lists in some cities can be long. It often helps to research and contact providers as early as possible. Timing varies by city and provider.

faq.q: Do children need vaccination records when relocating?
faq.a: Schools and childcare providers may ask for vaccination or health records. Having them organized — and translated if required — can smooth enrollment. Requirements vary by school and municipality.

faq.q: Can the whole family register at the same address?
faq.a: Usually yes. Often the main applicant registers first and dependants are then added to the same address. Permit and municipality rules can affect the order; confirm with your gemeente.

servicesIntro: Services often used by families during the move: temporary housing, banking, insurance comparison, connectivity, and school or childcare research.
